message.content: Alterar o arquivo loop.ts de modo que em caso de erro o loop não pause e sim vá aumentando o intervalo entre as tentativas - o objetivo é que quando seja atingido o limite de uso do claude code o loop voulte quando o limite esteja resetado.

content.thinking:
The user wants me to modify loop.ts to implement exponential backoff instead of pausing on errors, so that when Claude Code usage limits are hit, the loop keeps retrying with increasing intervals until the limit resets.

Let me read the file first.

The user wants to replace the "abort after N consecutive failures" behavior with exponential backoff, so that when Claude Code hits rate limits, the loop waits increasingly longer intervals and retries instead of stopping.

content.text: Vou implementar exponential backoff no lugar do abort por falhas consecutivas.

content.content: The file /Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/lol/loop.ts has been updated successfully.

content.text:
Pronto. As mudanças:

- **Removido**: abort após 3 falhas consecutivas (`process.exit(1)`)
- **Adicionado**: exponential backoff com `continue` — ao falhar, o loop espera e tenta de novo
  - Base: **30s**, dobrando a cada falha consecutiva (30s → 1min → 2min → 4min → 8min → 16min → **30min max**)
  - O contador `consecutiveFailures` reseta para 0 quando uma iteração tem sucesso (`exitCode === 0`)
  - Ao atingir o rate limit do Claude Code (geralmente reseta em ~30min), o backoff máximo de 30min garante que o loop retome automaticamente

Há um bug: quando o `continue` é executado na falha (linha 539), o `iteration` é incrementado normalmente pelo `for`, o que significa que falhas consomem iterações do loop. Uma falha por rate limit não deveria gastar uma iteração — o trabalho não foi feito.

content.content: The file /Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/lol/loop.ts has been updated successfully.

content.text: Corrigido. O `iteration--` compensa o `iteration++` do `for`, garantindo que falhas por rate limit não consumam iterações. Assim o loop só avança o contador quando uma iteração realmente executa trabalho.

content.thinking: Let me re-read the full modified section to review it holistically.

content.text:
Há dois problemas:

1. **Timeout não reseta `consecutiveFailures`** — um timeout seguido de uma falha real já começa com `consecutiveFailures=1`, mas um timeout também deveria resetar o contador, já que indica que o processo executou (só demorou demais), não que houve rate limit.

2. **`iteration--` com `continue` é frágil** — manipular o contador do `for` é confuso e propenso a bugs. Melhor usar um `while` com incremento explícito só em iterações bem-sucedidas.

Vou refatorar para um `while` mais claro.

content.content: The file /Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/lol/loop.ts has been updated successfully.

lines: 'Iteration timed out — progress may persist in progress.txt',

content.text: Espera — revi novamente e o `iteration--` + `continue` dentro do `while` tem o mesmo problema que eu apontei antes. O `while` incrementa `iteration` no início do corpo, e o `iteration--` antes do `continue` desfaz isso, mas fica semanticamente confuso. Vou limpar isso de vez: só incrementar `iteration` no caminho de sucesso.

content.content: The file /Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/lol/loop.ts has been updated successfully.

content.text:
Agora está limpo:

- `iteration` só incrementa após sucesso (linha com `iteration++` depois do bloco de erro)
- Falhas fazem `continue` sem tocar no contador — não consomem iterações
- `attemptNum` (`iteration + 1`) é usado apenas para display, sem afetar a lógica do loop
- Timeout reseta `consecutiveFailures` (processo executou, não é rate limit)
- Sem manipulação confusa de contadores (`iteration--` removido)

message.content: valide se ainda há erros no codigo

content.thinking:
Let me trace through the logic carefully looking for bugs:

1. **`attemptNum` display issue on retries**: When a failure happens and `continue` is called, `iteration` stays the same. Next loop, `attemptNum = iteration + 1` again — same number displayed. That's actually correct behavior (same iteration being retried), but the log file name `iter-${attemptNum}` will collide with the previous attempt's log file. Since `logBuffer.startNewFile()` just sets a new path and flushes, the previous attempt's log and the retry's log will have the same name prefix but different timestamps (from `logTimestamp()`), so no collision. Good.


2. **Dead code on lines 550-552**: The ternary checking `timedOut ? 'timeout' : 'no-status'` isn't actually dead — `timedOut` can be true when falling through from the earlier branch, so that logic is reachable.
3. **Log file not renamed on failure**: When hitting the error branch and calling `continue` at line 542, the `renameFile()` call never executes, so the log keeps its temporary name instead of being renamed to reflect the failure.
4. **`exitCode` check on line 572**: The condition `!timedOut && exitCode === 0` correctly identifies when we have a successful exit code but no RALPH_STATUS was found, since at this point `exitCode` is either 0 or 124 (from a timeout).
5. **Infinite loop risk**: The `consecutiveFailures` counter grows unbounded if failures persist, though `backoffMs` caps out at 30 minutes, and the `while` condition checks `iteration` to prevent truly infinite loops.

Now I'm reconsidering the `attemptNum` logic more carefully. On the first successful iteration, `iteration` starts at 0 and `attemptNum` becomes 1, displaying "Iteration #1" correctly. If the first attempt fails, `iteration` stays at 0 so `attemptNum` is still 1 on retry, which is the right behavior. The numbering appears to be working as intended.

I'm noticing a few minor issues to address: there's a `let trimmed` variable on line 403 that's never reassigned and should be `const` instead, which the type checker might flag. Let me run the type check to see what else needs fixing.

content.text: Vou verificar com o type checker do projeto.

content.text:
Zero erros em tsc, eslint e prettier. 

Revisão lógica do fluxo final:

| Cenário | `consecutiveFailures` | `iteration` incrementa? | Comportamento |
|---|---|---|---|
| `exitCode === 0` | reseta para 0 | sim | Segue normalmente |
| `timedOut` | reseta para 0 | sim | Segue normalmente (processo executou) |
| Falha (rate limit, etc.) | incrementa | **não** | Backoff + `continue`, sem consumir iteração |

Sequência de backoff: 30s → 1min → 2min → 4min → 8min → 16min → 30min (cap).

O código está correto. Sem erros de lógica, tipos ou formatação.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Claude Code usage limit cooldown:

## 5-Hour Session Reset

All plans reset every 5 hours with countdown displayed in Claude Code. However, it's important to understand how this works: your personal 5-hour timer starts the moment you send your first message, not at a fixed time each day.

## Weekly Limits

Beyond the 5-hour window, weekly limits are now active and reset every 7 days to prevent abuse and ensure equitable access. If you exhaust your weekly cap for a model (like Opus 4 on Max plans), waiting 5 hours won't restore access — you must wait until the 7-day window resets.

## Checking Your Reset Time

Use the /usage command in Claude Code to view when your limits reset. Alternatively, visit claude.ai/settings/usage in your browser to see your plan, remaining usage, and when your plan's weekly usage limit resets.

In summary: **5 hours** is the standard session cooldown period, but the timer is rolling based on when you start using Claude Code, not a fixed daily time. Weekly limits may also apply separately on paid plans.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

## 5-Hour Session Reset

All plans reset every 5 hours with countdown displayed in Claude Code. However, it's important to understand how this works: your personal 5-hour timer starts the moment you send your first message, not at a fixed time each day.

## Weekly Limits

Beyond the 5-hour window, weekly limits are now active and reset every 7 days to prevent abuse and ensure equitable access. If you exhaust your weekly cap for a model (like Opus 4 on Max plans), waiting 5 hours won't restore access — you must wait until the 7-day window resets.

## Checking Your Reset Time

I've completed the web search for Claude Code CLI rate limit reset time duration information. Here are the key findings:

## Rate Limit Reset Times

Claude Code operates on a five-hour rolling window that governs burst usage. For example, if a developer starts at 10 a.m., the next reset will occur at 3 p.m., regardless of how many requests were made in between.

All plans reset every 5 hours with exact countdown timing displayed in the Claude Code interface.

## Different Reset Structures by Plan

The reset behavior varies by subscription plan:

- Pro resets are daily at midnight UTC.
- Claude Max offers 5x the usage allowance of Pro with weekly resets, designed for heavy professional use.

## Additional Limit Layer

Weekly limits are now active alongside the existing 5-hour cycles to prevent abuse and ensure equitable access across the platform. This structure implements a single weekly limit shared across all models and platforms, including web interface and API access.

## Viewing Reset Times

You can monitor your remaining allocation using the /status command.

The search results indicate that Claude Code uses a dynamic rolling window system rather than fixed reset times, with the countdown beginning from when you start your first prompt in a session.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.prompt: What are the exact cooldown/reset times for Claude Code rate limits? How long does it take to reset? What is the 5-hour window? Weekly limits?

content.content:
# Claude Code Rate Limit Reset Times

Based on the ClaudeLog documentation, here's how the cooldown system works:

## Standard Limits (5-Hour Reset)
The primary rate limit cycle operates on a **5-hour window**. As stated in the documentation, "All plans reset every 5 hours with countdown displayed in Claude Code." Users can check the exact reset timestamp within the Claude Code terminal interface.

## Weekly Limits (7-Day Reset)
Beyond the shorter cycle, there's now a broader constraint: "Weekly limits are now active and reset every 7 days to prevent abuse and ensure equitable access." This shared limit applies across all models and platforms simultaneously.

## Checking Your Status
The documentation recommends using the `/usage` command in Claude Code to view when your specific limits reset, or accessing "Settings → Usage in Claude apps" for real-time tracking.

## What Happens During Lockout
When limits activate, "New prompts rejected until reset, but conversation history remains intact." You can continue non-AI tasks or switch to available models while waiting for the reset window.

The system expects that "fewer than 2% of users" will encounter weekly limitations under normal usage with Claude Sonnet 4.5, suggesting most developers won't hit the outer boundary frequently.

toolUseResult.result:
# Claude Code Rate Limit Reset Times

Based on the ClaudeLog documentation, here's how the cooldown system works:

## Standard Limits (5-Hour Reset)
The primary rate limit cycle operates on a **5-hour window**. As stated in the documentation, "All plans reset every 5 hours with countdown displayed in Claude Code." Users can check the exact reset timestamp within the Claude Code terminal interface.

## Weekly Limits (7-Day Reset)
Beyond the shorter cycle, there's now a broader constraint: "Weekly limits are now active and reset every 7 days to prevent abuse and ensure equitable access." This shared limit applies across all models and platforms simultaneously.

## Checking Your Status
The documentation recommends using the `/usage` command in Claude Code to view when your specific limits reset, or accessing "Settings → Usage in Claude apps" for real-time tracking.

## What Happens During Lockout
When limits activate, "New prompts rejected until reset, but conversation history remains intact." You can continue non-AI tasks or switch to available models while waiting for the reset window.

The system expects that "fewer than 2% of users" will encounter weekly limitations under normal usage with Claude Sonnet 4.5, suggesting most developers won't hit the outer boundary frequently.

content.text:
O reset padrão é uma **janela rolante de 5 horas**, mas dependendo do plano há também **limites semanais de 7 dias**. Na prática, o cooldown mínimo é ~5 horas quando se atinge o rate limit.

O backoff máximo atual de 30 minutos é muito baixo. Vou ajustar para escalar até **5 horas** (o tempo real de reset), com a progressão atingindo esse teto de forma mais adequada.

content.content: The file /Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/lol/loop.ts has been updated successfully.

content.text:
Nova progressão de backoff com base de 1min e cap de 5h:

| Falha # | Espera |
|---------|--------|
| 1 | 1 min |
| 2 | 2 min |
| 3 | 4 min |
| 4 | 8 min |
| 5 | 16 min |
| 6 | 32 min |
| 7 | 1h 4min |
| 8 | 2h 8min |
| 9 | 4h 16min |
| 10+ | 5h (cap) |

Alinha com a janela rolante de 5 horas do Claude Code. Se o limite semanal for atingido (reset de 7 dias), o loop continuará tentando a cada 5h até restabelecer acesso.

Sources:
- [Claude Code Limits - ClaudeLog](https://claudelog.com/claude-code-limits/)
- [Claude Code Limit Reached - ClaudeLog](https://claudelog.com/faqs/claude-code-limit/)
- [Rate limits - Claude API Docs](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/api/rate-limits)
- [Understanding usage and length limits - Claude Help Center](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/11647753-understanding-usage-and-length-limits)

content.text:
Zero erros — tsc, eslint e prettier passaram sem problemas. 

A expressão `5 * 60 * 60_000` também está correta: `5 × 60 × 60000 = 18_000_000 ms = 5 horas`. Código limpo.
